chunks.text: In 1929, the American astronomer Edwin Hubble discovered that the more distant a galaxy was
the faster it was moving away from us.
This showed that the entire universe was expanding.
If the universe is expanding now, then in the past everything must have been small
hot, and gathered into a single point.
From this idea, the theory of the " Big Bang " was born.
Today, the universe is believed to have begun about 13.
8 billion years ago, in the Big Bang.
In its first few minutes, hydrogen and helium were created
and over hundreds of millions of years, the first star ignited.
the American researchers Penzias and Wilson detected a weak microwave radiation coming evenly from every direction of the sky.
This is called the cosmic microwave background
and it is regarded as the afterglow of the Big Bang.
It became decisive evidence for the Big Bang theory.
Our Milky Way galaxy is said to hold between 200 and 400 billion stars.
In the universe, there are said to be roughly the same number of galaxy.
But 95 percent of the universe still remains a mystery to us.
Dark matter, which we cannot directly observe, and dark energy
which is accelerating the expansion of the universe.
To uncover the nature of these is the greatest challenge of modern physics.
The James Webb Space Telescope, launched in 2021
is now bringing to us the light of the earliest galaxies of the universe.
Light born 13.
8 billion years ago is finally reaching the earth.
